one_line_takeaway: A single-author position/systems paper proposes a local SLM-based 'Privacy Guard' that jointly performs prompt decomposition and tiered cloud routing, empirically showing on synthetic Faker-generated benchmarks that a 7B model can cut cloud token cost by ~45-47.6% but leaks secrets almost exclusively when unstructured 'Lazy' prompts bury Institutional secrets (33.6% leakage in that quadrant vs. 0% for Personal secrets), a failure that persists non-monotonically even at 30B parameters and is only reliably solved by a 70B model or a deterministic regex/NER pre-filter.
key_finding: On the 1,000-sample (3,250-secret) cloud benchmark with qwen2.5:7b, blended OpEx (token-cost) reduction was 45.0% and overall leakage rate was 12.9% (420/3,250 secrets leaked), but this was highly non-uniform: 100% redaction success (0 leaks) on all Personal secrets and on Institutional secrets in Expert-profile (concise) prompts, versus a 33.6% leakage rate (420/1,250 secrets) concentrated exclusively in the Lazy/Institutional quadrant where secrets were buried in long unstructured log dumps. On the earlier 40-sample benchmark, qwen2.5-7b-instruct suffered a 100% leakage rate (40/40 cases leaked at least one secret) despite excelling at intent extraction, illustrating a tension the authors term a conflict between semantic compression (APO) and syntactic scrubbing (redaction) in sub-8B models. Scaling model size did not monotonically fix this: qwen3-coder-30b on the hardest 50 Lazy/Institutional samples still leaked 54.4% (117/215 secrets); in the small exploratory needle-in-a-haystack test, leakage ranged non-monotonically from 0% (32B Qwen2.5-Coder, 70B Llama-3.3) to 33.3%-100% (7B, 8B, 30B, and even the 104B Command R+). A follow-up statistically-scaled test of llama-3.3-70b-instruct (6-bit) on all 50 Lazy/Institutional samples achieved 1.20% leakage (3/250). Task decomposition on a 118-sample, ~11,300-token-per-sample system-log dataset reduced projected cloud cost from $6.71 (monolithic baseline) to $0.16 (decomposed/routed), a 97.54% OpEx reduction. LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation (qwen3-coder-30b as judge/cloud model) preferred the APO-compressed response over the raw baseline in 85.0% of 40 cases (34 wins), with 10.0% baseline wins (4) and 5.0% ties, i.e., compression did not degrade and appeared to improve perceived answer quality. Latency for the 7B APO step was 0.67s (±0.17s). Expert-profile prompts saw 13.9%-27.7% token reduction; Lazy-profile prompts saw 53.9%-58.1% (±2.6 SD) reduction. LIFO context-compacting (Section 7.1) and TTFT-crossover-above-10k-tokens claims (Section 7.2) are presented as theoretical/analytical projections, not measured empirical results.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from the related work it surveys in Section 2, which covers FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, LiteLLM, prompt-compression work like LLMLingua/CompactPrompt, and privacy-attack papers like Nasr et al. and Staab et al.) is a single-author proposed system called the 'Dual-Vault' / 'Privacy Guard' architecture: a local, on-premise Small Language Model (SLM, 7B-14B class) orchestrator that (1) performs Automatic Prompt Optimisation (APO) to decompose a user prompt into focused sub-tasks, (2) acts as a 'Holistic Contextual Observer' that monitors the full conversational context (not just single turns) for 'emergent leakage' (inference-based privacy risk accumulating across a session), and (3) routes sanitised sub-prompts through a four-tier trust hierarchy (Tier 0 untrusted/free, Tier 1 commercial standard, Tier 2 geographically compliant, Tier 3 zero-trust/on-premise) depending on residual risk. The paper also proposes an informal 'Inseparability Theorem' (Section 3), asserting that the same semantic-compression operator that reduces token cost (OpEx) also reduces exposed sensitive entropy, with a proof sketch rather than a rigorous formal proof. It also proposes a LIFO-based context-compacting memory mechanism intended to bound working memory across long sessions ('Zero-Waste').
threat_model: The threat model is data exfiltration/leakage of sensitive information (PII, financial data, health data, corporate IP, cryptographic keys/infrastructure secrets) to third-party cloud LLM providers through prompt content, including both direct inclusion of secrets in a single prompt and 'emergent leakage' -- inference of sensitive attributes by the cloud model from accumulated, seemingly innocuous context across a multi-turn conversation (citing Staab et al. on attribute inference and Zhang et al./Chu et al. on conversation reconstruction/membership inference as the basis for this risk). The defender is assumed to control a trusted local/on-premise SLM used as a gatekeeper before any content reaches an untrusted cloud model; the cloud providers themselves (Tier 0-3) are treated as adversarial or semi-trusted according to tier. The paper explicitly does NOT model prompt-injection or jailbreak attacks against the SLM orchestrator itself, nor adversarial manipulation of the routing decision -- its threat model is purely about outbound content leakage, not about attacks on the guard mechanism.
limitations: (1) The 'Inseparability Theorem' (Section 3) is an informal, unproven mathematical framing -- it defines quantities like 'sensitive entropy Es(P)' without a concrete measurement procedure and the 'proof sketch' is qualitative reasoning, not a rigorous derivation; the paper itself calls it a proof sketch. (2) The core empirical claims rest on a synthetic, Faker-library-generated dataset with injected secrets rather than real user prompts or real-world sensitive data, limiting ecological validity. (3) Leakage-rate results for models beyond the primary 7B model (30B, 32B, 70B, 104B) are explicitly described by the authors as based on limited/exploratory sample sizes ('not statistically exhaustive... designed to identify structural vulnerabilities rather than provide exhaustive statistical baselines'), and the reported leakage rates are non-monotonic and somewhat erratic across parameter scale, undermining any clean scaling narrative. (4) Cost figures ($6.71 vs $0.16) are described as 'projected'/simulated costs rather than measured real API billing. (5) The LIFO memory-compacting mechanism and the latency-crossover analysis (Section 7) are explicitly analytical/theoretical projections, not benchmarked implementations. (6) Answer-quality evaluation relies on LLM-as-a-Judge with a single judge model (qwen3-coder-30b) on only 40 samples, a small and potentially self-referential evaluation (the same model class used elsewhere in the pipeline). (7) The work is single-author, uses AI assistance for manuscript editing/bibliography/benchmark scripts (disclosed in Acknowledgements), and the threat model excludes adversarial attacks on the Privacy Guard/orchestrator itself (e.g., prompt injection to bypass the local SLM's redaction), meaning the system's own attack surface is not evaluated.

datasets_used: Synthetic Faker-library-generated dataset: 40 samples / 140 injected secrets (60 Personal, 80 Institutional), 2x2 matrix (Expert/Lazy x Personal/Institutional)
Scaled synthetic dataset: 1,000 samples / 3,250 injected secrets, same 2x2 matrix, run on NVIDIA T4 GPU (Google Colab) via Ollama with qwen2.5:7b at Q4_0 quantisation
50-sample subset of the hardest Lazy/Institutional quadrant (used for qwen3-coder-30b and llama-3.3-70b-instruct 6-bit tests)
Exploratory single-prompt needle-in-a-haystack test: one 60-line synthetic server-log dump with deeply buried secrets, evaluated across 6 models (8B-104B)
118-sample simulated massive-system-log dataset (~11,300 input tokens/sample) for the task-decomposition OpEx benchmark
40-sample subset used for the LLM-as-a-Judge answer-quality comparison
quantitative_results: 1,000-sample/3,250-secret benchmark: 45.0% blended OpEx reduction; 12.9% overall leakage rate (420/3,250 secrets leaked)
100% redaction success (0 leaks) on all Personal secrets and on Institutional secrets in Expert-profile prompts
33.6% leakage rate (420/1,250 secrets) in the Lazy/Institutional quadrant specifically
40-sample benchmark with qwen2.5-7b-instruct: 100% leakage rate (40/40 cases leaked at least one secret)
APO token reduction: 13.9%-27.7% for Expert-profile prompts; 53.9%-58.1% (±2.6 SD) for Lazy-profile prompts
APO latency: 0.67s (±0.17s) per call on local hardware
qwen3-coder-30b on 50 hardest Lazy/Institutional samples: 54.4% leakage rate (117/215 secrets leaked)
Exploratory needle-in-a-haystack leakage by model: Qwen2.5-7B 100%, Llama-3.1-8B 33.3%, Qwen3-Coder-30B 54.4%, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B 0.0%, Llama-3.3-70B 0.0%, Command-R+-104B 33.3%
llama-3.3-70b-instruct (6-bit) on all 50 Lazy/Institutional samples: 1.20% leakage rate (3/250 secrets leaked)
Task-decomposition cost benchmark (118 samples, ~11,300 tokens each): baseline monolithic cost $6.71 vs. Dual-Vault decomposition cost $0.16, i.e. 97.54% OpEx reduction
LLM-as-a-Judge answer quality (40 samples, qwen3-coder-30b judge/cloud model): Dual-Vault (APO) wins 85.0% (34), Baseline wins 10.0% (4), Ties 5.0% (2)
Hybrid architecture projection (deterministic filter + 8B SLM): 0% leakage, 45.0% OpEx reduction, at ~1/10th VRAM of a 70B-class deployment
baselines_compared: Baseline (Monolithic) cloud-only routing vs. Dual-Vault decomposition/routing (cost comparison)
Raw/uncompressed prompt responses vs. APO-compressed prompt responses (LLM-as-a-Judge quality comparison)
qwen2.5-7b-instruct vs. qwen3-coder-30b vs. llama-3.1-8b vs. qwen2.5-coder-32b vs. llama-3.3-70b-instruct vs. command-r-plus-104b (parameter-scale leakage comparison)
SLM-only (8B class) vs. Presidio (deterministic regex/NER) vs. Frontier LLM (70B class) vs. Hybrid (Presidio + 8B) architecture comparison (leakage rate, OpEx reduction, VRAM cost)
